data.title: Top 5 Dividend Stocks For 2025
data.description:
Dividends: Thinking Beyond the January Effect

When it comes to Quant, the holidays are a time for reflection—to take stock of the past 12 months (pun intended) and set goals accordingly.

For many investors, it’s also a time to feel optimistic. For decades, hopeful stockpickers have clung to the theory that stocks tend to rise more in January than any other month, known as ‘The January Effect’. Several factors could be behind this phenomenon: The yearly December sell-off, in which investors cut their losses to minimize capital gains tax liabilities, followed by a wave of buying activity in January.

Other explanations could include the flood of cash into the market as companies hand out annual bonuses, end-of-year contributions to employee retirement plans, or a temporary spike in activity as investors implement financial New Year’s resolutions. After all, a majority of Americans, 62%, own stocks as of 2022, according to a 2023 Gallup poll.

Markets are already looking rosy—the Nasdaq and S&P 500 closed on record highs earlier this week, riding the wave of November’s substantial gains. Whether or not the rally continues into 2025, investors ought to be thinking far longer-term.

The New Year brings fresh uncertainty: the dollar has continued to rally since Donald Trump’s presidential victory as investors bet that his plan to introduce aggressive tariffs will prove inflationary, weighing on the Fed’s ability to slash interest rates.

Allow me to suggest a different way to combat inflation worries, prolonged high interest rates, and other impending risks: dividend-paying stocks.

Stocks with solid dividend growth offer investors a reliable income stream and can help hedge against potential inflation and market volatility. Plus, through the power of compounding, reinvesting dividends can significantly enhance the value of one's portfolio far beyond the next few months.

Global Dividend Payments Reached a Q3 Record of $431.1bn, up 3.1% Year-Over-Year

2025: The Year of the US Dividend?

With record cash on corporate balance sheets and near-record low payout ratios, the market is in a prime position to foster dividend growth and consistent payouts. According to Janus Henderson, global dividends hit a record $431.1B in the third quarter of 2024, up 3.1% year-on-year on a headline and underlying basis, with banks and media companies among the most significant contributors.

In the US, Q3 dividend growth was 10.0% on an underlying basis, more than double the global average. Contributions by Meta and Alphabet, both of which paid dividends for the first time this year, helped power that lead.

As I wrote back in August, dividend-paying stocks are primed for potential rate cuts as investors search for yield that has eased in bond markets. My three dividend recommendations from August 26, 2024, ALEX, INGR, and SYF, are up on average 15.8% vs. the S&P 500 8.1%. The cheaper cost of debt frees up more cash that can be reinvested into businesses or paid out in dividends, creating potential opportunities for investors.

SA Quant has identified five new dividend stocks with an average forward yield of 6.48% offering high-yield opportunities with strong dividend growth and safety characteristics, excellent financials, great factor grades, and Quant “Strong Buy” recommendations. As the Fed continues its attempts to decrease rates into 2025, it could be beneficial to stock up on sectors that tend to outperform in declining rate environments including Real Estate, Financials, and Consumer stocks—all of which are touched upon in the selection below.

The S&P 500’s dividend yield has been stabilizing over the past two decades

Seeking Alpha’s Dividend Factor Grades

Seeking Alpha's Quant Dividend Grading System can help you meet your investing goals by assessing each stock's dividend strengths and weaknesses compared to its sector in real time to produce key performance measures, including Dividend Safety Grades and Dividend Growth Grades.

The SA Quant team has identified five dividend-paying stocks well-positioned to generate reliable income in the face of potential volatility. While all of these stocks offer favorable dividend yield, they are also evaluated along with other dividend grades, leveraging a sophisticated data-driven approach to offer a reliable assessment of a company’s ability to keep paying its dividends and avoid dividend cuts.

Seeking Alpha’s strong buy growth recommendations have beat the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG) 12 years running

Similarly, the dividend growth grade provides an instant characterization of a company’s ability to grow its dividends based on data-driven analysis. This tool is valuable for income-focused investors who want to pinpoint companies with better dividend growth potential.

1. Getty Realty Corp. (GTY)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.78B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Dividend Yield: 5.8%

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 3 out of 178

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 1 out of 25

Ranked first among SA’s Top Real Estate Stocks, Getty Realty offers an attractive proposition to long-term dividend investors. Its impressive 5.75% dividend yield is driven higher by a stable business model focused on retail and automotive services, offering steady cash flow and consistent growth. Expectations of interest rate cuts could benefit the company's model in the long term by freeing up capital.

The company, which maintains properties in 42 states and Washington, D.C, raised its quarterly dividend by 4.4% to $0.47/share in Q3, keeping up its ‘A’ Dividend Consistency Grade with 10 years of consistent dividend growth and payouts, respectively.

As SA analyst Seeking Profits previously noted, GTY’s portfolio of convenience stores, car washes, and auto parts stores has weathered the storms of the COVID-19 pandemic and the rise of online shopping. The company also focuses on long-term leases, contributing to its 99.7% occupancy rate. According to its Q3 earnings report, the company is focused on growth, with $70.0M invested for future acquisitions of 24 convenience and automotive retail properties.

GTY dividend scorecard

GTY has maintained solid dividend growth over the years, and has strong five-year and 10-year CAGR dividend growth rates of 5.15% and 8.45%, respectively. Forward revenue growth of 8.46% suggests the potential for future consistent returns, and its above-average interest coverage (TTM) ratio of 2.76 indicates that it consistently covers interest rate payments.

GTY is also highly profitable, with an ‘A+’-rated EBITDA margin (TTM) of 79.28%, a 45.87% difference from the sector, and a gross profit margin (TTM) of 91.32%, a 39.17% difference from the sector.

As I’ve written before, REITs perform well in a lower-rate environment due to cheaper debt costs and more attractive dividend yields relative to bonds, which boost demand and share prices. My next dividend-friendly suggestion hails from the consumer sector, which enjoys many of the same low-rate benefits as real estate.

2. British American Tobacco (BTI)

  • Market Capitalization: $82.11B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Dividend Yield: 8.05%

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 5 out of 187

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 1 out of 8

There appears to be a consensus among analysts that Republicans’ return to the White House will benefit the tobacco industry. Ranked the #1 Quant pick among the Top Tobacco Stocks and boasting an impressive dividend yield of nearly 8%, British American Tobacco is ahead of the curve—particularly for dividend-inclined investors.

The maker of Lucky Strike cigarettes among other brands has gained 28% year-to-date, riding the wave of next-generation products (NGPs) such as vapes, e-cigarettes, and pouches. Despite this rally, which rivals the ascents of the S&P and Nasdaq in what has been a boon of a year for the stock market, BTI shares are significantly undervalued. Its ‘A’-rated forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) of 8.64–55.75% lower than the rest of the sector–paired with its 8.02% trailing dividend yield suggests that BTI shares come at an attractive discount.

The British multinational is aiming for its cigarette alternatives to account for 50% of its revenues by 2035, a strategy that has resonated with both equity and bond investors, SA contributor Dividend Sensei highlights. The company began selling 40-year bonds, yielding 4%, in 2015. Factor in 5% rate hikes by the Fed, and those bonds yield 6.4% today–meaning investors are confident that BTI will still generate strong free cash flow in 2055 even as cigarettes become a more minor part of its business.

BTI dividend scorecard

With an ‘A+’ Dividend Yield Grade, BTI has proven its ability to reward its shareholders with lucrative payouts. Standout metrics include its four-year average dividend yield of 7.95%, a 226.90% difference from the sector, and an operating earnings yield of 16.88%, a 92.43% difference from the sector.

Strong one-, three-, and five-year yield on cost ratios, which measure a stock's current dividend by the price initially paid, suggest that shareholders are getting significant bang for their buck.

Dividend growth is also a significant selling point for BTI, with an earnings per share (EPS) diluted growth rate of 9.93%—50% higher than the sector’s 6% median—indicating strong potential for long-term growth.

3. VICI Properties (VICI)

  • Market Capitalization: $33.49B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Dividend Yield: 5.45%

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 7 out of 178

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 1 out of 11

Ranked #1 in the world of Specialized REITs, VICI Properties’ portfolio of Vegas strongholds including Caesars Palace, MGM Grand, and The Venetian, and track record of consistent returns proves to be an easy gamble for investors.

With an impressive 5.45% dividend yield, the property group made my list of 14 must-have dividend stocks and Top REITs in October. It remains high on my list today thanks to its consistent dividend growth and ‘A+’ Growth and Profitability Quant ratings.

Formed in 2017 as a spin-off from Caesars Entertainment Corporation as part of the entertainment group’s bankruptcy process, VICI has since evolved into a highly diversified empire of casinos, restaurants, bars, golf courses, racetracks, and more. Its growth strategy, which prioritizes consistent dividend increases, helped the company weather periods of volatility including the COVID-19 pandemic while placing shareholders at the forefront.

VICI’s Q3 earnings left shareholders feeling lucky despite lagging the market in recent years. Net income attributable to stockholders was up 31.7% to $732.9M, total revenues were up 6.7% year over year to $964.7M, and the quarterly cash dividend of $0.4325 per share increased 4.2%.

VICI dividend scorecard

VICI has charted steady Dividend Growth over the past five years, with a solid five-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 7.66%, a 959.31% difference from the 0.74% sector median. Its outstanding earnings per share diluted growth rate of 29.19%—leaps ahead of the sector’s -5.33% median—underscores VICI’s attractiveness for long-term investors and the company’s potential for strong dividend growth down the line.

Forward funds from operations growth of 28.05% compared to the sector median of 2.16%—a 1,201.22% difference—earns VICI an ‘A+’ in terms of its future cash flow outlook.

VICI also comes at an attractive price tag. The stock trades at a deep discount with a forward P/E ratio of 11.85x and an EV/EBIT (TTM) of 14x, which are 65.82% and 59.74% below the sector median, respectively.

And with compelling Dividend Safety Metrics, including an ‘A’-rated dividend payout ratio (TTM) of 62.13%—a -52.49% difference from the sector—and a one-year dividend payout ratio of 63.27%—a -52.68% difference—VICI once again proves that the house always wins.

4. NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP)

  • Market Capitalization: $416.25M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Dividend Yield: 9.01%

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 4 out of 178

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 1 out of 13

NewLake Capital Partners may have a market cap of less than half a billion, but here at the SA Quant team, we have high hopes for this triple-net lease REIT.

With an incredible 9.01% dividend yield, more than double the sector median, the property group specializing in leasing to state-licensed cannabis operators makes for an attractive bet even in a business challenged by federal cannabis restrictions.

“With the cannabis industry projected to grow at a 10.5% CAGR through 2027,” SA contributor Shariq Khan observes, “the company is well-positioned to benefit from the increasing demand for real estate capital as more states expand their markets.”

NLCP dividend scorecard

For dividend-focused investors, NLCP’s stellar Dividend Yield and Dividend Safety grades suggest that the company is highly dedicated to returning value to its shareholders. Its four-year dividend yield of 7.43%—a 72.53% difference from the sector median of 4.31%—and one-year yield on cost ratio of 12.56%—a 157.66% difference from the sector median of 4.87%—reflect consistently lucrative investor payouts. The company’s 6.65% forward earnings yield and 6.86% trailing earnings yield, a 144.80% and 147.83% difference from the sector, further support that theory.

Its ‘A+’-rated interest coverage (TTM) ratio of 33.36x, a 1,677.14% difference from the sector median of 1.88, and Net Income/Total Debt ratio of 355.91%, a 6,081.08% difference from the sector median of 5.76%, further suggest that NLCP can be relied on for steady dividends.

5. Portland General Electric Company (POR)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.93B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Dividend Yield: 4.28%

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 7 out of 107

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/5/24): 5 out of 42

Oregon’s clean energy mandates will require 100% renewable electricity by 2040. To meet those mandates, Portland General Electric Company is in the midst of a sweeping growth strategy, focusing on solar power, hydro power, and exiting coal plants. It is also expanding its grid to help fulfill the data center needs of the state’s fast-growing semiconductor and tech industries.

With a dividend yield of 4.28%, POR offers investors both income and growth potential. With 8 positive earnings per share (EPS) revisions in the last 90 days, analysts also seem convinced, with POR projected to grow 31.46% YoY by the end of 2024.

POR dividend scorecard

With 17 years of consistent dividend growth and payments, POR’s Consistency Grade is among its major selling points. It also offers strong dividend metrics across the board regarding safety, growth, and yield.

Worth a closer look is the dividend yield to dividend payout ratio (TTM), which reflects the portion of company earnings paid to shareholders. At 7.42%, a 43.12% difference from the sector median, the figure suggests that POR has consistently placed investors as a top priority.

When it comes to growth, POR’s forward EBIT growth rate of 15.45%, a 54.76% difference from the 9.98% sector median, reflects a steady increase in its earnings before interest and taxes over time.

Conclusion

There is nothing wrong with starting out the New Year feeling optimistic about where the market is headed. But embracing dividend-paying stocks can help keep your investing New Year’s resolutions on track, especially for investors that focus on income-generation securities.

Dividend-paying stocks can enhance portfolio diversification and stability, helping to position your investments for success in 2025 and beyond, including potential benefits from the 'January Effect.'

The stocks featured here can help complement a diverse portfolio. Alternatively, if you want a consistent flow of monthly stock ideas beyond this article, consider exploring Alpha Picks if you would like access to Seeking Alpha Quant’s best monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
